key_insight: A split-head Quantum GAN that separates lattice-level and atom-level generation improves material discovery by combining thermodynamic precision with broader structural diversity.
engineering_relevance: This architecture offers a more resource-efficient path for generating candidate crystalline materials, especially where classical generative models struggle with mode collapse and 3D spatial constraints.
method: Use a physics-informed split-head design from the quantum trunk to decouple lattice bounds from atomic coordinates.
Train and compare the SH-QGAN against an architecture-matched classical ablation model.
Evaluate candidate structures on the constrained Mg-Mn-O composition space for validity, diversity, and stoichiometric convergence.
evidence_signal: On the Mg-Mn-O system, the classical ablation was more thermodynamically precise, while the SH-QGAN more than doubled geometric validity and produced novel metastable candidates near Mg2MnO4 stoichiometry.
watch_item: Results are shown on a constrained materials system, so broader generalization and hardware practicality may depend on the target chemistry and quantum circuit capacity.
paper_title: Split-Head Quantum Generative Adversarial Network for Crystalline Material Discovery
